QuestionText: Which of the following BEST describes the difference between supervised and unsupervised learning?
Explanation: Supervised learning uses labeled training data where the correct output is known for each input—the model learns to map inputs to outputs. Unsupervised learning uses unlabeled data and discovers hidden patterns or structures without predefined answers. Examples: supervised includes classification and regression; unsupervised includes clustering and dimensionality reduction.
Option1: Supervised learning uses labeled data while unsupervised learning finds patterns in unlabeled data
Option2: Supervised learning requires more compute than unsupervised learning
Option3: Unsupervised learning always produces more accurate results
Option4: There is no meaningful difference between them

QuestionText: A company wants to predict whether a customer will churn (yes/no) based on their usage patterns. Which type of ML problem is this?
Explanation: Binary classification predicts one of two discrete outcomes (churn/no churn). The model learns from historical data where the churn outcome is known (labeled data) and applies those patterns to predict future churn. This is supervised learning because the training data includes the correct answer (label) for each example.
Option1: Binary classification
Option2: Regression
Option3: Clustering

QuestionText: Which of the following are examples of supervised learning tasks? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Spam email detection is classification (supervised)—the model learns from emails labeled as spam or not-spam. Predicting house prices is regression (supervised)—the model learns from houses with known sale prices. Customer segmentation is unsupervised (clustering). Anomaly detection can be unsupervised. Both spam detection and price prediction require labeled training data.
Option1: Spam email detection (classification)
Option2: Predicting house prices (regression)
Option3: Customer segmentation without predefined groups
Option4: Discovering topics in a collection of documents

QuestionText: A data scientist notices their model performs extremely well on training data but poorly on new unseen data. What is this problem called?
Explanation: Overfitting occurs when a model learns the training data too well—including noise and outliers—rather than the underlying pattern. The model essentially memorizes the training data instead of generalizing. Signs include high training accuracy but low test/validation accuracy. Solutions include regularization, more training data, simpler models, dropout, and cross-validation.
Option1: Overfitting
Option2: Underfitting
Option3: Data leakage

QuestionText: Which AWS service provides a fully managed environment for building training and deploying machine learning models?
Explanation: Amazon SageMaker is a fully managed ML platform that covers the entire ML lifecycle. It provides Jupyter notebooks for development, built-in algorithms, distributed training, hyperparameter tuning, model hosting with auto-scaling, and MLOps capabilities including model monitoring and pipelines. SageMaker removes the heavy lifting from each step of the ML workflow.
Option1: Amazon SageMaker
Option2: AWS Lambda
Option3: Amazon Comprehend

QuestionText: A company wants to add image recognition capabilities to their application without building a custom ML model. Which AWS service should they use?
Explanation: Amazon Rekognition provides pre-trained computer vision capabilities including object detection, facial analysis, text in images, celebrity recognition, and content moderation. It requires no ML expertise—you simply call the API with an image and receive structured results. This is an AI service (not an ML platform), designed for developers who want to add vision capabilities without training models.
Option1: Amazon Rekognition
Option2: Amazon SageMaker
Option3: Amazon Textract

QuestionText: Which of the following metrics is MOST appropriate for evaluating a classification model where the cost of false positives is very high?
Explanation: Precision measures the proportion of positive predictions that are actually correct (true positives / (true positives + false positives)). When false positives are costly (e.g., flagging legitimate transactions as fraud, causing customer friction), high precision ensures that when the model predicts positive, it is very likely correct. Recall would be prioritized when false negatives are more costly.
Option1: Precision
Option2: Recall
Option3: F1 Score

QuestionText: Which of the following are common steps in a typical ML pipeline? (Select THREE)
Explanation: Data preparation (cleaning, feature engineering) transforms raw data into a format suitable for training. Model training fits the algorithm to the prepared data. Model evaluation assesses performance on held-out test data using appropriate metrics. These three steps form the core of any ML pipeline, often iterated multiple times to improve results.
Option1: Data preparation and feature engineering
Option2: Model training
Option3: Model evaluation on test data

QuestionText: A company wants to extract text and structured data from scanned documents and forms. Which AWS service should they use?
Explanation: Amazon Textract uses ML to automatically extract text, handwriting, and structured data (tables, forms) from scanned documents. Unlike simple OCR, Textract understands the structure of documents—it identifies form fields and their values, table rows and columns, and maintains the spatial relationships between elements. No ML expertise is required.
Option1: Amazon Textract
Option2: Amazon Comprehend
Option3: Amazon Translate

QuestionText: What is the purpose of splitting data into training validation and test sets in machine learning?
Explanation: The training set is used to train the model. The validation set is used during training to tune hyperparameters and prevent overfitting (the model never trains on this data). The test set is held out completely and used only for final evaluation to estimate real-world performance. This three-way split ensures an unbiased estimate of how the model will perform on new data.
Option1: Training data trains the model; validation data tunes hyperparameters; test data provides final unbiased evaluation
Option2: All three sets are used interchangeably during training
Option3: The test set is used to train the model and the training set evaluates it
Option4: Splitting data is only necessary for unsupervised learning

QuestionText: Which of the following AWS AI services can analyze text to determine sentiment language and key phrases? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Amazon Comprehend is a natural language processing (NLP) service that analyzes text for sentiment (positive, negative, neutral, mixed), language detection, key phrase extraction, entity recognition, and topic modeling. Amazon Comprehend Medical is specialized for healthcare text. Both use pre-trained models requiring no ML expertise.
Option1: Amazon Comprehend for general text analysis
Option2: Amazon Comprehend Medical for healthcare text
Option3: Amazon Polly for text analysis

QuestionText: A company wants to build a chatbot that understands natural language and can handle customer service inquiries. Which AWS service should they use?
Explanation: Amazon Lex provides the building blocks for conversational interfaces (chatbots and voice bots). It uses the same technology as Alexa and provides automatic speech recognition (ASR) and natural language understanding (NLU). Lex handles intent recognition, slot filling, and dialog management, integrating with Lambda for business logic fulfillment.
Option1: Amazon Lex
Option2: Amazon Polly
Option3: Amazon Transcribe

QuestionText: Which type of machine learning involves an agent learning to make decisions by receiving rewards or penalties for its actions?
Explanation: Reinforcement learning (RL) involves an agent that learns to make decisions by interacting with an environment. The agent takes actions, receives rewards or penalties, and learns a policy that maximizes cumulative reward over time. Examples include game playing, robotics, and recommendation systems. AWS DeepRacer uses RL to train autonomous racing models.
Option1: Reinforcement learning
Option2: Supervised learning
Option3: Unsupervised learning

QuestionText: A company wants to convert text to natural-sounding speech for their application. Which AWS service should they use?
Explanation: Amazon Polly converts text into lifelike speech using deep learning. It supports multiple languages and voices, including Neural Text-to-Speech (NTTS) for the most natural-sounding output. Polly can generate speech in real-time for interactive applications or batch-generate audio files. It supports SSML tags for controlling pronunciation, volume, and speaking rate.
Option1: Amazon Polly
Option2: Amazon Transcribe
Option3: Amazon Lex

QuestionText: Which of the following statements about feature engineering is TRUE?
Explanation: Feature engineering is the process of using domain knowledge to create, transform, or select input variables (features) that improve model performance. Good features capture the relevant patterns in data. Examples include creating a 'day of week' feature from a timestamp, normalizing numerical values, or encoding categorical variables. It is often the most impactful step in ML.
Option1: It is the process of creating and selecting input variables that improve model performance using domain knowledge
Option2: It is only necessary for deep learning models
Option3: It is automatically handled by all ML algorithms

QuestionText: Which AWS service converts speech to text in real-time?
Explanation: Amazon Transcribe is an automatic speech recognition (ASR) service that converts audio to text. It supports real-time streaming transcription and batch processing of audio files. Transcribe handles multiple languages, speaker identification, custom vocabularies, and automatic punctuation. It is used for call center analytics, meeting transcription, and subtitle generation.
Option1: Amazon Transcribe
Option2: Amazon Polly
Option3: Amazon Translate

QuestionText: What is a foundation model?
Explanation: A foundation model is a large AI model trained on broad data at scale that can be adapted (fine-tuned) to a wide range of downstream tasks. Examples include GPT, Claude, Llama, and Stable Diffusion. Foundation models learn general representations from massive datasets and can perform tasks they were not explicitly trained for through prompting or fine-tuning, making them versatile building blocks for AI applications.
Option1: A large AI model trained on broad data that can be adapted to many downstream tasks
Option2: A small model trained for a single specific task
Option3: A database schema for storing AI training data
Option4: A hardware architecture for running ML workloads

QuestionText: Which AWS service provides access to foundation models from multiple providers through a single API?
Explanation: Amazon Bedrock is a fully managed service that provides access to foundation models from AI21 Labs, Anthropic, Cohere, Meta, Mistral AI, Stability AI, and Amazon through a single API. Bedrock allows you to experiment with different models, customize them with your data, and build generative AI applications without managing infrastructure. It supports text, image, and embedding models.
Option1: Amazon Bedrock
Option2: Amazon SageMaker JumpStart
Option3: Amazon Comprehend

QuestionText: Which of the following BEST describes how a large language model (LLM) generates text?
Explanation: LLMs generate text by predicting the next token (word or subword) in a sequence based on the preceding context. The model assigns probabilities to all possible next tokens and selects one (using sampling strategies like temperature, top-k, or top-p). This process repeats token by token until a stopping condition is met. The model's knowledge comes from patterns learned during pre-training on massive text corpora.
Option1: By predicting the next token in a sequence based on learned patterns from training data
Option2: By searching a database of pre-written responses
Option3: By copying text directly from the internet in real-time

QuestionText: What is the purpose of prompt engineering?
Explanation: Prompt engineering is the practice of designing and refining input prompts to guide a foundation model toward producing desired outputs. Effective prompts provide clear instructions, relevant context, examples (few-shot learning), and constraints. Good prompt engineering can dramatically improve output quality without modifying the model itself, making it a cost-effective way to optimize AI application performance.
Option1: Designing input prompts to guide a foundation model toward producing desired outputs
Option2: Training a new model from scratch
Option3: Modifying the model's neural network weights
Option4: Building the hardware infrastructure for AI

QuestionText: Which of the following are common prompt engineering techniques? (Select THREE)
Explanation: Zero-shot prompting provides instructions without examples. Few-shot prompting includes examples of desired input-output pairs in the prompt. Chain-of-thought prompting asks the model to show its reasoning step by step, improving accuracy on complex tasks. All three are widely used techniques that improve model output quality without changing the model itself.
Option1: Zero-shot prompting with clear instructions only
Option2: Few-shot prompting with example input-output pairs
Option3: Chain-of-thought prompting for step-by-step reasoning

QuestionText: What is 'hallucination' in the context of generative AI?
Explanation: Hallucination occurs when a generative AI model produces output that sounds plausible but is factually incorrect, fabricated, or not grounded in the provided context. LLMs generate text based on statistical patterns, not factual understanding, so they can confidently state false information. Mitigation strategies include Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG), grounding with source data, and human review.
Option1: When a model generates plausible-sounding but factually incorrect or fabricated information
Option2: When a model refuses to generate any output
Option3: When a model generates output too slowly
Option4: When a model copies text verbatim from training data

QuestionText: What is Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)?
Explanation: RAG combines a foundation model with an external knowledge retrieval system. When a user asks a question, the system first retrieves relevant documents from a knowledge base (using vector search), then provides those documents as context to the foundation model along with the question. This grounds the model's response in actual data, reducing hallucinations and enabling responses based on current, domain-specific information.
Option1: A technique that retrieves relevant documents from a knowledge base and provides them as context to a foundation model
Option2: A method for training models on larger datasets
Option3: A technique for compressing model size
Option4: A way to generate random training data

QuestionText: What is the purpose of embeddings in the context of generative AI?
Explanation: Embeddings are numerical vector representations of text (or images, audio) that capture semantic meaning. Similar concepts have similar embeddings (close in vector space). Embeddings enable semantic search—finding documents related to a query by meaning rather than keyword matching. They are essential for RAG systems, where relevant documents are retrieved by comparing embedding similarity.
Option1: Numerical vector representations that capture semantic meaning enabling similarity-based search and retrieval
Option2: Compressed versions of images for faster loading
Option3: Encryption keys for securing AI models

QuestionText: Which of the following are types of generative AI models? (Select THREE)
Explanation: Large Language Models (LLMs) generate text (GPT, Claude, Llama). Diffusion models generate images from text descriptions (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E). Multimodal models process and generate multiple types of content—text, images, audio, video (GPT-4V, Claude 3). All three are distinct categories of generative AI with different architectures and capabilities.
Option1: Large Language Models (LLMs) for text generation
Option2: Diffusion models for image generation
Option3: Multimodal models for processing multiple content types

QuestionText: What is fine-tuning in the context of foundation models?
Explanation: Fine-tuning adapts a pre-trained foundation model to a specific task or domain by training it further on a smaller, task-specific dataset. The model's weights are updated to improve performance on the target task while retaining general knowledge from pre-training. Fine-tuning requires less data and compute than training from scratch. Amazon Bedrock supports fine-tuning for several foundation models.
Option1: Adapting a pre-trained model to a specific task by training further on task-specific data
Option2: Training a model from scratch on a new dataset
Option3: Adjusting the hardware configuration for better performance
Option4: Removing unwanted data from the training set

QuestionText: What is the 'temperature' parameter in LLM inference?
Explanation: Temperature controls the randomness of the model's output. A low temperature (e.g., 0.1) makes the model more deterministic—it strongly favors the highest-probability tokens, producing focused and consistent output. A high temperature (e.g., 0.9) increases randomness—the model considers lower-probability tokens, producing more creative and diverse output. Temperature 0 always selects the most likely token.
Option1: A parameter that controls the randomness and creativity of the model's output
Option2: The physical temperature of the GPU during inference
Option3: The speed at which the model generates tokens
Option4: The amount of memory allocated to the model

QuestionText: Which of the following statements about tokens in LLMs is TRUE?
Explanation: Tokens are the basic units that LLMs process. A token can be a word, part of a word (subword), or a character, depending on the tokenizer. For example, 'unhappiness' might be split into 'un', 'happi', 'ness'. The context window (maximum number of tokens the model can process) limits how much text can be provided as input and generated as output in a single interaction.
Option1: Tokens are the basic units LLMs process and can be words subwords or characters
Option2: Each token always represents exactly one complete word
Option3: Tokens are only used during model training not inference

QuestionText: A company wants to build a generative AI application that answers questions about their internal documentation. Which approach is MOST appropriate?
Explanation: RAG with Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases is the most appropriate approach. The company's documentation is ingested, chunked, and stored as embeddings in a vector database. When a user asks a question, the system retrieves relevant document chunks and provides them as context to the foundation model. This grounds responses in the company's actual documentation, reducing hallucinations and keeping answers current.
Option1: Implement RAG using Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases with the company's documentation
Option2: Fine-tune a foundation model on all company documentation
Option3: Train a custom model from scratch
Option4: Use a general-purpose LLM without any company context

QuestionText: What is the difference between inference and training in the context of AI?
Explanation: Training is the process of teaching a model by exposing it to data and adjusting its parameters (weights) to minimize errors—this is computationally expensive and done once or periodically. Inference is using the trained model to make predictions or generate outputs on new inputs—this happens in real-time when users interact with the model. Training creates the model; inference uses it.
Option1: Training teaches the model by adjusting weights on data; inference uses the trained model to generate outputs on new inputs
Option2: Training and inference are the same process
Option3: Inference requires more compute than training

QuestionText: What is a vector database used for in generative AI applications?
Explanation: Vector databases store and efficiently search high-dimensional embedding vectors. In RAG applications, documents are converted to embeddings and stored in a vector database. When a query arrives, its embedding is compared against stored embeddings using similarity search (cosine similarity, dot product) to find the most relevant documents. Amazon OpenSearch Serverless and Amazon Aurora support vector search.
Option1: Storing and efficiently searching embedding vectors for similarity-based retrieval in RAG applications
Option2: Storing relational data in tables with rows and columns
Option3: Compressing AI models for faster inference
Option4: Managing user authentication for AI applications

QuestionText: Which of the following are valid strategies to reduce hallucinations in generative AI? (Select TWO)
Explanation: RAG grounds the model's responses in retrieved factual documents, reducing fabrication. Lowering the temperature makes the model more deterministic, favoring high-probability (more likely correct) tokens over creative but potentially inaccurate ones. Both strategies reduce hallucinations from different angles—RAG provides factual grounding while low temperature reduces randomness.
Option1: Use Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) to ground responses in factual documents
Option2: Lower the temperature parameter for more deterministic outputs
Option3: Increase the temperature for more creative outputs
Option4: Remove all context from the prompt

QuestionText: A company needs to select a foundation model for a customer support chatbot. Which TWO factors are MOST important when evaluating models? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Response latency directly impacts user experience in real-time chat—users expect fast responses. Domain relevance ensures the model understands customer support language, terminology, and patterns. Cost, accuracy, and latency for the specific task should be evaluated through benchmarking before committing to production. Model size alone doesn't determine suitability.
Option1: Response latency for real-time interaction
Option2: Relevance to customer support domain
Option3: Total number of model parameters only

QuestionText: A company wants to build a generative AI application using Amazon Bedrock. Which of the following can they do with Bedrock? (Select THREE)
Explanation: Bedrock provides access to multiple foundation models through a single API for text generation, summarization, and conversation. Bedrock Knowledge Bases enables RAG by connecting models to your data sources. Bedrock Agents allow models to take actions by calling APIs and accessing tools. Bedrock also supports fine-tuning, guardrails, and model evaluation.
Option1: Access multiple foundation models through a single API
Option2: Build RAG applications using Bedrock Knowledge Bases
Option3: Create AI agents that can call APIs and use tools
Option4: Train foundation models from scratch on custom hardware

QuestionText: A company wants to summarize long customer support transcripts into brief action items. Which generative AI capability is this?
Explanation: Text summarization is a core capability of LLMs. The model reads the full transcript and generates a concise summary highlighting key points and action items. This is an extractive/abstractive summarization task. The prompt should specify the desired output format (bullet points, action items) and length constraints. Amazon Bedrock models excel at this task with appropriate prompting.
Option1: Text summarization
Option2: Image generation
Option3: Speech recognition

QuestionText: What is an AI agent in the context of Amazon Bedrock?
Explanation: Amazon Bedrock Agents are AI systems that can reason about user requests, break them into steps, and take actions by calling APIs, querying databases, or invoking Lambda functions. Agents use a foundation model for reasoning and planning, combined with action groups that define available tools. This enables AI applications that go beyond text generation to actually perform tasks on behalf of users.
Option1: An AI system that reasons about requests and takes actions by calling APIs and tools
Option2: A human customer service representative
Option3: A monitoring tool for AI model performance
Option4: A database for storing AI training data

QuestionText: A company wants to generate product descriptions automatically from product attributes (name category features). Which approach should they use?
Explanation: A foundation model accessed through Amazon Bedrock can generate product descriptions from structured attributes. The prompt includes the product attributes and instructions for the desired tone, length, and style. Few-shot prompting (including examples of good descriptions) improves output quality. This can be automated at scale by calling the Bedrock API for each product.
Option1: Use a foundation model via Amazon Bedrock with a prompt containing product attributes and style instructions
Option2: Use Amazon Rekognition to generate text from product images
Option3: Use Amazon Translate to create descriptions
Option4: Use Amazon Polly to generate product descriptions

QuestionText: Which of the following are valid use cases for generative AI in business? (Select THREE)
Explanation: Content generation (marketing copy, emails, reports) is a primary use case for LLMs. Code generation and assistance helps developers write, debug, and explain code. Customer service automation uses chatbots powered by foundation models to handle inquiries. All three are proven business applications of generative AI that deliver measurable value.
Option1: Content generation for marketing and communications
Option2: Code generation and developer assistance
Option3: Customer service chatbot automation

QuestionText: A company wants to implement semantic search over their knowledge base so users can find information by meaning rather than exact keywords. Which technology enables this?
Explanation: Embeddings convert text into numerical vectors that capture semantic meaning. Similar concepts have similar vectors. By embedding all documents in the knowledge base and the user's query, semantic search finds documents whose embeddings are closest to the query embedding—matching by meaning rather than keywords. This is powered by embedding models available through Amazon Bedrock.
Option1: Text embeddings with vector similarity search
Option2: Traditional keyword-based full-text search
Option3: SQL database queries with LIKE operators

QuestionText: What is the purpose of Amazon Bedrock Guardrails?
Explanation: Amazon Bedrock Guardrails provides configurable safeguards for generative AI applications. You can define content filters (blocking harmful content), denied topics (preventing the model from discussing specific subjects), word filters, sensitive information filters (detecting and redacting PII), and contextual grounding checks (reducing hallucinations). Guardrails are applied to both inputs and outputs.
Option1: To provide configurable content filters and safety controls for generative AI applications
Option2: To improve the speed of model inference
Option3: To reduce the cost of API calls

QuestionText: A company wants to build a generative AI application that can process both text and images in the same conversation. Which type of model should they use?
Explanation: Multimodal models can process and understand multiple types of input—text, images, audio, and video—in a single interaction. Models like Anthropic Claude 3 (available on Bedrock) accept both text and image inputs, enabling use cases like describing images, analyzing charts, reading documents with visual elements, and visual question answering.
Option1: A multimodal foundation model that accepts both text and image inputs
Option2: A text-only LLM with a separate image processing pipeline
Option3: An image generation model like Stable Diffusion

QuestionText: Which of the following describes the correct flow of a RAG application?
Explanation: In RAG: (1) The user's query is converted to an embedding. (2) The embedding is used to search a vector database for relevant documents. (3) Retrieved documents are added to the prompt as context. (4) The foundation model generates a response grounded in the retrieved context. This flow ensures responses are based on actual data rather than the model's training data alone.
Option1: User query → embedding → vector search → retrieved context added to prompt → model generates grounded response
Option2: User query → model generates response → search for supporting documents → append documents to response
Option3: User query → fine-tune model → generate response
Option4: User query → keyword search → return raw documents without model generation

QuestionText: A company wants to use generative AI to analyze and extract information from PDF documents containing text tables and images. Which Amazon Bedrock capability should they use?
Explanation: Multimodal foundation models on Bedrock (like Claude 3) can process PDF documents containing mixed content—text, tables, charts, and images. The model understands the visual layout and can extract specific information, summarize content, answer questions about the document, and convert unstructured document data into structured formats. This eliminates the need for separate OCR and NLP pipelines.
Option1: Use a multimodal foundation model on Amazon Bedrock to process the PDF documents directly
Option2: Use Amazon Textract only for all document understanding
Option3: Manually transcribe the documents before processing
Option4: Use Amazon Rekognition for document analysis

QuestionText: What is the purpose of the 'system prompt' in a conversation with a foundation model?
Explanation: The system prompt sets the model's behavior, persona, and constraints for the entire conversation. It defines who the model is (e.g., 'You are a helpful customer service agent for Acme Corp'), what it should and shouldn't do, its tone, and any specific instructions. The system prompt persists across all user messages in the conversation, providing consistent behavior.
Option1: To set the model's behavior persona and constraints for the entire conversation
Option2: To provide the user's first question
Option3: To configure the model's hardware resources

QuestionText: A company wants to fine-tune a foundation model on Amazon Bedrock using their proprietary data. Which of the following is TRUE about fine-tuning on Bedrock?
Explanation: Bedrock fine-tuning (custom model training) uses your data to adapt a foundation model's behavior. Your training data remains private—it is not used to train the base model or shared with the model provider. The fine-tuned model is exclusive to your account. Bedrock handles the infrastructure, and you pay for the training compute and inference on the custom model.
Option1: Your training data remains private and is not shared with the model provider or used to improve the base model
Option2: Fine-tuning requires you to manage GPU instances
Option3: Fine-tuned models are shared with all Bedrock customers

QuestionText: Which of the following are benefits of using Amazon Bedrock compared to self-hosting foundation models? (Select THREE)
Explanation: Bedrock is serverless—no infrastructure to manage, provision, or scale. It provides access to multiple model providers through a single API, enabling easy experimentation. Built-in security features include encryption, VPC endpoints, and no data sharing with model providers. These benefits reduce operational overhead and accelerate time-to-market for generative AI applications.
Option1: No infrastructure management (serverless)
Option2: Access to multiple model providers through a single API
Option3: Built-in security with encryption and data privacy
Option4: Lower quality models compared to self-hosting

QuestionText: A company wants to build an AI-powered search experience that understands natural language questions and returns precise answers from their documentation. Which architecture should they use?
Explanation: A RAG architecture with Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases ingests the documentation, creates embeddings, and stores them in a vector database. When a user asks a natural language question, the system retrieves relevant document chunks via semantic search and provides them to the foundation model, which generates a precise answer with citations to the source documents.
Option1: RAG with Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases for semantic search and answer generation
Option2: Traditional keyword search with Elasticsearch
Option3: A custom-trained classification model
Option4: Amazon Lex chatbot without any knowledge base

QuestionText: What is 'prompt injection' and why is it a concern?
Explanation: Prompt injection is an attack where malicious input is crafted to override the model's system prompt or instructions, causing it to behave in unintended ways—like revealing system prompts, ignoring safety guidelines, or generating harmful content. It is a concern because it can bypass application controls. Mitigation includes input validation, Bedrock Guardrails, and output filtering.
Option1: An attack where malicious input overrides the model's instructions causing unintended behavior
Option2: A technique for improving model accuracy
Option3: A method for compressing model size

QuestionText: A company wants to use generative AI to automatically generate code based on natural language descriptions. Which of the following is TRUE?
Explanation: Foundation models like Amazon CodeWhisperer (now Amazon Q Developer) and models on Bedrock can generate code from natural language descriptions. The generated code should always be reviewed by a developer for correctness, security vulnerabilities, and adherence to coding standards. AI-generated code is a productivity tool, not a replacement for human judgment and code review.
Option1: AI can generate code from natural language but generated code should always be reviewed by developers for correctness and security
Option2: AI-generated code is always correct and never needs review
Option3: AI cannot generate functional code

QuestionText: Which of the following are components of an Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base? (Select TWO)
Explanation: A data source (S3 bucket containing documents) provides the raw content to be indexed. A vector store (Amazon OpenSearch Serverless, Aurora, Pinecone, or Redis) stores the document embeddings for similarity search. Bedrock Knowledge Bases handles the ingestion pipeline—chunking documents, generating embeddings, and storing them in the vector store automatically.
Option1: A data source containing the documents to be indexed
Option2: A vector store for storing and searching document embeddings
Option3: A relational database for storing user queries
Option4: A GPU cluster for model training

QuestionText: Which of the following are key principles of responsible AI? (Select THREE)
Explanation: Fairness ensures AI systems do not discriminate against individuals or groups based on protected characteristics. Transparency means users understand how AI decisions are made and that they are interacting with AI. Accountability means organizations take responsibility for AI system outcomes and have processes to address issues. These are foundational principles across all responsible AI frameworks.
Option1: Fairness and non-discrimination
Option2: Transparency and explainability
Option3: Accountability for AI outcomes

QuestionText: What is AI bias and how can it occur?
Explanation: AI bias occurs when a model produces systematically unfair outcomes for certain groups. It can originate from biased training data (historical discrimination reflected in data), biased labeling (human annotators' prejudices), selection bias (non-representative training samples), or algorithmic bias (model architecture amplifying existing patterns). Bias can lead to discriminatory outcomes in hiring, lending, healthcare, and criminal justice.
Option1: Systematic unfairness in AI outputs caused by biased training data labeling or algorithms
Option2: A feature that makes AI models more accurate
Option3: A hardware limitation of GPU processors

QuestionText: A company is deploying a generative AI chatbot for customer service. Which of the following responsible AI practices should they implement?
Explanation: All of these are essential responsible AI practices. Disclosing that users are interacting with AI (transparency) builds trust. Implementing content filters prevents harmful outputs. Providing a way to escalate to a human agent ensures users aren't trapped in unhelpful AI loops. Monitoring outputs for bias and quality ensures ongoing responsible operation. Together they form a comprehensive responsible AI approach.
Option1: Disclose to users that they are interacting with AI
Option2: Implement content filters to prevent harmful outputs
Option3: Provide an option to escalate to a human agent

QuestionText: What is 'explainability' in the context of AI and why is it important?
Explanation: Explainability is the ability to understand and communicate how an AI model arrives at its decisions or outputs. It is important for building trust with users, meeting regulatory requirements (like GDPR's right to explanation), debugging model errors, identifying bias, and ensuring accountability. More explainable models enable stakeholders to verify that decisions are fair and reasonable.
Option1: The ability to understand and communicate how an AI model makes decisions important for trust regulation and accountability
Option2: The speed at which a model generates output
Option3: The amount of training data used

QuestionText: Which of the following are strategies to mitigate bias in AI systems? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Using diverse and representative training data ensures the model learns from a broad range of examples, reducing the risk of systematic bias against underrepresented groups. Regular bias auditing and testing across different demographic groups identifies disparate impact before deployment. Both are proactive strategies that address bias at different stages of the ML lifecycle.
Option1: Use diverse and representative training data
Option2: Regularly audit and test models for bias across demographic groups
Option3: Use only data from a single demographic group
Option4: Ignore bias since AI is inherently objective

QuestionText: A company is using AI to screen job applications. Which responsible AI concern is MOST critical?
Explanation: Fairness and non-discrimination is the most critical concern for AI-powered hiring. Historical hiring data may reflect past discrimination (gender, race, age bias), and a model trained on this data could perpetuate or amplify those biases. The company must ensure the model does not discriminate based on protected characteristics, regularly audit for disparate impact, and maintain human oversight of hiring decisions.
Option1: Ensuring the model does not discriminate based on protected characteristics like gender race or age
Option2: Ensuring the model processes applications quickly
Option3: Ensuring the model uses the latest AI technology

QuestionText: What is the purpose of human-in-the-loop (HITL) in AI systems?
Explanation: Human-in-the-loop keeps humans involved in AI decision-making processes, especially for high-stakes decisions. Humans review, validate, or override AI outputs before they are acted upon. This provides a safety net against AI errors, bias, and hallucinations. HITL is essential in healthcare, legal, financial, and other domains where incorrect AI decisions could have serious consequences.
Option1: To keep humans involved in reviewing and validating AI decisions especially for high-stakes applications
Option2: To replace all human workers with AI
Option3: To slow down AI processing for cost savings

QuestionText: Which of the following is an example of AI transparency?
Explanation: Informing users that a response was generated by AI (not a human) is a fundamental transparency practice. It allows users to calibrate their trust appropriately—they may want to verify AI-generated information or request human assistance. Transparency also includes explaining how the AI system works at a high level and what data it was trained on.
Option1: Clearly labeling AI-generated content so users know they are interacting with AI
Option2: Hiding the fact that AI is being used from customers
Option3: Using the most complex model available regardless of explainability

QuestionText: A company wants to ensure their generative AI application does not produce harmful toxic or inappropriate content. Which Amazon Bedrock feature helps with this?
Explanation: Amazon Bedrock Guardrails allows you to configure content filters that block harmful, toxic, violent, sexual, or otherwise inappropriate content in both model inputs and outputs. You can set filter strengths for different content categories, define denied topics, add word filters, and configure PII detection. Guardrails act as a safety layer between users and the foundation model.
Option1: Amazon Bedrock Guardrails with content filtering
Option2: Amazon CloudWatch for content monitoring
Option3: AWS WAF for content filtering

QuestionText: Which of the following statements about responsible AI governance is TRUE?
Explanation: Responsible AI governance requires ongoing monitoring and evaluation—not just a one-time assessment. AI systems can drift over time as data distributions change, new biases emerge, or the operating context evolves. Continuous monitoring of model performance, fairness metrics, and user feedback ensures the system remains responsible throughout its lifecycle.
Option1: Responsible AI requires ongoing monitoring and evaluation throughout the AI system's lifecycle not just at deployment
Option2: Responsible AI only needs to be considered during model training
Option3: Once deployed AI systems do not need further governance

Category: Security Compliance and Governance for AI Solutions
QuestionText: A company is using Amazon Bedrock and wants to ensure that their data is not used to train the foundation models. Is this possible?
Explanation: Yes. Amazon Bedrock does not use customer inputs or outputs to train the base foundation models. Your data remains private to your account. Bedrock encrypts data in transit and at rest, supports VPC endpoints for private connectivity, and provides CloudTrail logging for all API calls. This data privacy guarantee is a core feature of the Bedrock service.
Option1: Yes Bedrock does not use customer data to train base foundation models and all data remains private to your account
Option2: No all data sent to Bedrock is used to improve the models
Option3: Only data from enterprise accounts is kept private

Category: Security Compliance and Governance for AI Solutions
QuestionText: Which of the following security features does Amazon Bedrock provide? (Select THREE)
Explanation: Bedrock encrypts all data in transit using TLS and at rest using AWS KMS keys. VPC endpoints (AWS PrivateLink) allow you to access Bedrock without traffic traversing the public internet. CloudTrail logs all Bedrock API calls for auditing. Together these provide comprehensive security for generative AI workloads, meeting enterprise security requirements.
Option1: Encryption of data in transit and at rest
Option2: VPC endpoints for private connectivity via AWS PrivateLink
Option3: CloudTrail logging of all API calls for auditing
Option4: Public access to all model weights

Category: Security Compliance and Governance for AI Solutions
QuestionText: A company needs to ensure that their AI application complies with GDPR requirements for personal data processing. Which of the following should they implement? (Select TWO)
Explanation: GDPR requires that personal data processing has a lawful basis and that individuals can exercise their rights (access, deletion, correction). Using Bedrock Guardrails to detect and redact PII prevents personal data from being inadvertently processed or stored by the AI system. Maintaining audit logs (CloudTrail) of all AI interactions provides the accountability and traceability GDPR requires.
Option1: Configure Bedrock Guardrails to detect and redact PII in inputs and outputs
Option2: Maintain audit logs of all AI interactions using CloudTrail
Option3: Store all personal data indefinitely without consent
Option4: Disable all encryption for easier data access

Category: Security Compliance and Governance for AI Solutions
QuestionText: Which AWS service provides logging and monitoring of all API calls made to Amazon Bedrock?
Explanation: AWS CloudTrail records all API calls made to Amazon Bedrock, including who made the call, when, from where (source IP), what parameters were used, and the response. This provides a complete audit trail for compliance, security analysis, and troubleshooting. CloudTrail logs can be stored in S3 and analyzed with Athena or CloudWatch Logs Insights.
Option1: AWS CloudTrail
Option2: Amazon CloudWatch Metrics only
Option3: AWS Config

Category: Security Compliance and Governance for AI Solutions
QuestionText: A company wants to control which foundation models their developers can access on Amazon Bedrock. Which AWS service should they use to enforce this?
Explanation: IAM policies can restrict which Bedrock models, actions, and resources users and roles can access. For example, a policy can allow access to only specific model IDs (like anthropic.claude-3-sonnet) while denying access to others. SCPs in AWS Organizations can enforce these restrictions across all accounts. This provides fine-grained access control over AI model usage.
Option1: AWS IAM policies to restrict access to specific Bedrock models and actions
Option2: Amazon Bedrock Guardrails
Option3: AWS WAF

Category: Security Compliance and Governance for AI Solutions
QuestionText: Which of the following are governance considerations when deploying generative AI in an enterprise? (Select THREE)
Explanation: Data privacy ensures customer and proprietary data is protected when used with AI models. Model access control determines who can use which models and for what purposes. Cost management tracks and controls AI spending across teams. All three are essential governance considerations that enterprises must address before deploying generative AI at scale.
Option1: Data privacy and protection
Option2: Model access control and permissions
Option3: Cost management and usage tracking
Option4: Allowing unrestricted access to all models for all employees

Category: Security Compliance and Governance for AI Solutions
QuestionText: A company wants to ensure that sensitive information like credit card numbers and social security numbers is not included in prompts sent to foundation models. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: Amazon Bedrock Guardrails includes sensitive information filters that detect and redact PII (personally identifiable information) like credit card numbers, SSNs, phone numbers, and email addresses in both inputs and outputs. This prevents sensitive data from being sent to the model or included in responses, helping maintain data privacy and regulatory compliance.
Option1: Configure Amazon Bedrock Guardrails with sensitive information filters to detect and redact PII
Option2: Trust users to manually remove sensitive information
Option3: Disable the AI application for users who handle sensitive data

Category: Security Compliance and Governance for AI Solutions
QuestionText: What is model governance in the context of enterprise AI?
Explanation: Model governance encompasses the policies, processes, and controls for managing AI models throughout their lifecycle—from selection and evaluation through deployment, monitoring, and retirement. It includes model inventory (tracking which models are used where), access controls, performance monitoring, bias auditing, cost tracking, and compliance documentation. Good governance ensures AI is used responsibly and effectively.
Option1: Policies processes and controls for managing AI models throughout their lifecycle including selection deployment monitoring and compliance
Option2: The process of training AI models
Option3: The hardware infrastructure for running AI models

Category: Security Compliance and Governance for AI Solutions
QuestionText: A company is evaluating the security of using Amazon Bedrock for their generative AI application. Which of the following statements is TRUE?
Explanation: Amazon Bedrock provides enterprise-grade security. Customer data is encrypted in transit (TLS) and at rest (KMS). Data is not shared with model providers or used to train base models. VPC endpoints keep traffic private. IAM controls access. CloudTrail provides audit logging. Bedrock is compliant with SOC, ISO, HIPAA, and other standards. All of these security features are available by default or configurable.
Option1: Customer data is encrypted is not shared with model providers and Bedrock supports compliance standards like SOC ISO and HIPAA
Option2: Customer data is shared with model providers to improve models
Option3: Bedrock does not support any compliance certifications

Category: Security Compliance and Governance for AI Solutions
QuestionText: Which of the following is a security best practice when building generative AI applications on AWS?
Explanation: All of these are security best practices. Using least-privilege IAM roles limits what the AI application can access. Enabling CloudTrail logging provides an audit trail. Using VPC endpoints keeps traffic private. Implementing Guardrails filters harmful content. Together they form a defense-in-depth security approach for generative AI applications.
Option1: Use least-privilege IAM roles for the AI application
Option2: Enable CloudTrail logging for all Bedrock API calls
Option3: Use VPC endpoints for private Bedrock access

QuestionText: Which of the following is an example of unsupervised learning?
Explanation: Customer segmentation groups customers based on purchasing behavior without predefined labels. The algorithm discovers natural clusters in the data. This is unsupervised because there are no 'correct' group assignments provided during training—the model finds the structure on its own. K-means clustering and hierarchical clustering are common algorithms for this task.
Option1: Grouping customers into segments based on purchasing behavior without predefined labels
Option2: Predicting whether an email is spam or not spam
Option3: Training a self-driving car with reward signals
Option4: Classifying images as cats or dogs using labeled examples

QuestionText: What is transfer learning and why is it important for AI?
Explanation: Transfer learning takes a model trained on one task (usually a large general dataset) and adapts it to a different but related task. The pre-trained model has already learned useful features that transfer to the new task, requiring much less data and compute for fine-tuning. Foundation models are the ultimate example—trained on massive data then adapted to countless downstream tasks.
Option1: Reusing a model trained on one task and adapting it to a different related task requiring less data and compute
Option2: Transferring data between AWS Regions
Option3: Moving a model from one programming language to another

QuestionText: A company wants to detect fraudulent transactions in real-time. Which type of ML approach is MOST appropriate?
Explanation: Anomaly detection (which can be supervised or semi-supervised) identifies transactions that deviate from normal patterns. For fraud detection, the model learns what normal transactions look like and flags outliers. Amazon SageMaker has built-in algorithms for anomaly detection (Random Cut Forest). Real-time inference endpoints can score transactions as they occur.
Option1: Anomaly detection to identify transactions that deviate from normal patterns
Option2: Image classification
Option3: Text summarization

QuestionText: Which of the following AWS AI services require NO machine learning expertise to use? (Select THREE)
Explanation: Amazon Rekognition (image/video analysis), Amazon Comprehend (text analysis/NLP), and Amazon Transcribe (speech-to-text) are all pre-trained AI services accessed via simple API calls. They require no ML expertise—developers send data and receive structured results. These services abstract away all model training, tuning, and infrastructure management.
Option1: Amazon Rekognition for image analysis
Option2: Amazon Comprehend for text analysis
Option3: Amazon Transcribe for speech-to-text
Option4: Amazon SageMaker for custom model training

QuestionText: What is the difference between AI ML and deep learning?
Explanation: AI is the broadest concept—any system that mimics human intelligence. ML is a subset of AI where systems learn from data without being explicitly programmed. Deep learning is a subset of ML using neural networks with many layers (deep neural networks) that can learn complex patterns. Each is contained within the previous: AI > ML > Deep Learning.
Option1: AI is the broadest concept; ML is a subset of AI that learns from data; deep learning is a subset of ML using multi-layer neural networks
Option2: They are three different names for the same technology
Option3: Deep learning is broader than AI

QuestionText: What is the context window of a large language model?
Explanation: The context window is the maximum number of tokens (input + output) that an LLM can process in a single interaction. It determines how much text you can provide as input (including system prompt, conversation history, and retrieved documents) and how much the model can generate. Larger context windows enable processing longer documents and maintaining longer conversations.
Option1: The maximum number of tokens an LLM can process in a single interaction including both input and output
Option2: The physical display size of the AI application
Option3: The time window during which the model is available
Option4: The number of users who can access the model simultaneously

QuestionText: Which of the following are valid methods to customize a foundation model's behavior WITHOUT modifying its weights? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Prompt engineering crafts input prompts to guide the model's behavior—including system prompts, few-shot examples, and specific instructions. RAG provides external knowledge as context in the prompt. Neither method modifies the model's weights—they work by providing better input. Fine-tuning and continued pre-training DO modify weights and are therefore not correct answers here.
Option1: Prompt engineering with system prompts and few-shot examples
Option2: Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) to provide external knowledge
Option3: Fine-tuning on custom data

QuestionText: What is 'grounding' in the context of generative AI?
Explanation: Grounding connects a model's outputs to verifiable sources of information. In RAG, the model is grounded by providing retrieved documents as context—the model generates responses based on this factual context rather than relying solely on its training data. Grounding reduces hallucinations and enables citations. Amazon Bedrock Guardrails includes contextual grounding checks.
Option1: Connecting model outputs to verifiable sources of information to reduce hallucinations and enable factual responses
Option2: Installing the model on physical hardware
Option3: Connecting the model to the internet

QuestionText: A company is comparing foundation models for their use case. Which of the following factors should they consider? (Select THREE)
Explanation: Model accuracy/quality for the specific task determines whether the model produces useful outputs. Latency affects user experience—some models respond faster than others. Cost per token (input and output) directly impacts the total cost of ownership. All three should be evaluated through benchmarking on representative examples before selecting a model for production.
Option1: Model accuracy and quality for the specific use case
Option2: Inference latency and response time
Option3: Cost per token for input and output

QuestionText: What is the difference between zero-shot and few-shot prompting?
Explanation: Zero-shot prompting provides only instructions without examples—the model relies entirely on its pre-trained knowledge. Few-shot prompting includes examples of desired input-output pairs in the prompt, showing the model the expected format and behavior. Few-shot typically produces better results for complex or specific tasks because the examples demonstrate exactly what is expected.
Option1: Zero-shot provides instructions only; few-shot includes examples of desired input-output pairs in the prompt
Option2: Zero-shot uses zero compute resources; few-shot uses a few
Option3: They are different names for the same technique

QuestionText: What is 'chunking' in the context of RAG applications?
Explanation: Chunking is the process of splitting large documents into smaller segments (chunks) before creating embeddings. Chunk size affects retrieval quality—too large and irrelevant content dilutes the signal; too small and important context is lost. Common strategies include fixed-size chunks with overlap, sentence-based splitting, and semantic chunking. Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases handles chunking automatically.
Option1: Splitting large documents into smaller segments before creating embeddings for optimal retrieval in RAG
Option2: Compressing model weights for faster inference
Option3: Dividing compute resources across multiple GPUs

QuestionText: Which of the following BEST describes the relationship between tokens and cost in generative AI?
Explanation: Most generative AI services (including Amazon Bedrock) charge based on the number of input tokens (your prompt) and output tokens (the model's response). More tokens = higher cost. Input and output tokens often have different prices. Optimizing prompts to be concise, using shorter system prompts, and limiting output length all reduce costs.
Option1: Cost is directly proportional to the number of input and output tokens processed
Option2: Cost is based on the number of API calls regardless of token count
Option3: Longer prompts are always cheaper than shorter ones

QuestionText: A company wants to use generative AI to classify customer support tickets into categories (billing technical shipping). Which approach should they use?
Explanation: A foundation model can classify text into predefined categories through prompt engineering. The prompt includes the categories, classification instructions, and the ticket text. The model returns the appropriate category. This is faster and cheaper than training a custom classification model, and can be easily updated by modifying the prompt when categories change.
Option1: Use a foundation model with a classification prompt that includes the categories and ticket text
Option2: Train a custom deep learning model from scratch
Option3: Use Amazon Rekognition for text classification

QuestionText: What is Amazon Q and how does it relate to generative AI?
Explanation: Amazon Q is a generative AI-powered assistant for business and development. Amazon Q Business connects to enterprise data sources and provides answers grounded in company knowledge. Amazon Q Developer helps with coding tasks including code generation, debugging, and transformation. Both use foundation models with RAG to provide contextually relevant, grounded responses.
Option1: A generative AI assistant for business (Q Business) and development (Q Developer) that provides grounded responses using RAG
Option2: A message queuing service like Amazon SQS
Option3: A database query optimization tool

QuestionText: A company wants to generate images from text descriptions for their marketing materials. Which Amazon Bedrock model capability should they use?
Explanation: Image generation models like Stability AI's Stable Diffusion (available on Amazon Bedrock) and Amazon Titan Image Generator create images from text descriptions (text-to-image). You provide a text prompt describing the desired image, and the model generates it. These models can also perform image-to-image transformations, inpainting, and outpainting.
Option1: Use an image generation model on Amazon Bedrock like Stable Diffusion or Amazon Titan Image Generator
Option2: Use Amazon Rekognition to generate images
Option3: Use Amazon Polly to create visual content
Option4: Use Amazon Textract for image generation

QuestionText: Which of the following are benefits of using RAG over fine-tuning for incorporating company-specific knowledge? (Select TWO)
Explanation: RAG uses current data from the knowledge base—when documents are updated, the RAG system immediately reflects the changes without retraining. RAG is also more cost-effective because it doesn't require the compute expense of fine-tuning a model. Fine-tuning is better for changing the model's behavior or style, while RAG is better for providing factual, up-to-date knowledge.
Option1: RAG uses current data that can be updated without retraining the model
Option2: RAG is more cost-effective than fine-tuning
Option3: RAG permanently changes the model's weights
Option4: RAG requires more training data than fine-tuning

QuestionText: A company wants to build a generative AI application that can access their CRM system to look up customer information during conversations. Which Amazon Bedrock feature enables this?
Explanation: Amazon Bedrock Agents can be configured with action groups that define tools the agent can use—including calling APIs, querying databases, or invoking Lambda functions. When a user asks about a customer, the agent reasons that it needs CRM data, calls the appropriate action group (Lambda function that queries the CRM), and incorporates the results into its response.
Option1: Amazon Bedrock Agents with action groups that call the CRM system via Lambda
Option2: Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases
Option3: Amazon Bedrock Guardrails

QuestionText: What is the purpose of the 'top-p' (nucleus sampling) parameter in LLM inference?
Explanation: Top-p (nucleus sampling) controls output diversity by considering only the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds p. For example, top-p=0.9 considers tokens that together account for 90% of the probability mass, ignoring the long tail of unlikely tokens. Lower top-p values produce more focused output; higher values allow more diversity. It works alongside temperature.
Option1: It controls output diversity by limiting token selection to those within a cumulative probability threshold
Option2: It sets the maximum number of output tokens
Option3: It determines the number of parallel processing threads

QuestionText: A company wants to evaluate different foundation models on Amazon Bedrock for their specific use case. Which Bedrock feature helps with this?
Explanation: Amazon Bedrock Model Evaluation allows you to compare foundation models using automatic evaluation (built-in metrics like accuracy, robustness, toxicity) or human evaluation (custom criteria rated by human reviewers). You provide a test dataset and evaluation criteria, and Bedrock runs the evaluation across selected models, producing comparison reports to inform model selection.
Option1: Amazon Bedrock Model Evaluation for comparing models with automatic and human evaluation
Option2: AWS Trusted Advisor for model comparison
Option3: Amazon CloudWatch for model benchmarking

QuestionText: Which of the following are valid approaches to reduce the cost of using foundation models on Amazon Bedrock? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Provisioned Throughput on Bedrock provides a committed capacity at a discounted rate compared to on-demand pricing—ideal for predictable, high-volume workloads. Optimizing prompts to be concise reduces the number of input tokens processed, directly lowering per-request costs. Both strategies reduce costs from different angles—commitment discounts and usage optimization.
Option1: Use Provisioned Throughput for predictable high-volume workloads
Option2: Optimize prompts to reduce token count
Option3: Use the largest available model for all tasks
Option4: Disable all safety features to reduce processing

QuestionText: A company is deploying an AI system that makes loan approval decisions. Which of the following responsible AI requirements should they meet? (Select THREE)
Explanation: Loan decisions directly impact people's lives, making responsible AI critical. The system must be fair (no discrimination by race, gender, age). Decisions must be explainable (applicants have a right to understand why they were denied). Human oversight ensures edge cases and appeals are handled appropriately. All three are legal and ethical requirements for AI in financial services.
Option1: Ensure the model does not discriminate based on protected characteristics
Option2: Provide explanations for loan denial decisions
Option3: Maintain human oversight for appeals and edge cases
Option4: Maximize approval rates regardless of risk

QuestionText: What is 'model drift' and why is it a responsible AI concern?
Explanation: Model drift occurs when a model's performance degrades over time because the real-world data distribution changes from what the model was trained on. For example, customer behavior may shift due to economic changes, making a churn prediction model less accurate. Drift is a responsible AI concern because degraded performance can lead to unfair or incorrect decisions without anyone noticing.
Option1: Performance degradation over time as real-world data distributions diverge from training data leading to potentially unfair decisions
Option2: Physical movement of AI hardware between data centers
Option3: Changes in the model's source code

QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a principle of responsible AI?
Explanation: Maximizing output speed at the expense of accuracy is not a responsible AI principle—it prioritizes performance over correctness, which can lead to harmful outcomes. Responsible AI principles include fairness, transparency, accountability, privacy, safety, and reliability. Speed is an engineering consideration but should never compromise the quality and safety of AI outputs.
Option1: Maximizing output speed at the expense of accuracy and safety
Option2: Fairness and non-discrimination
Option3: Transparency and explainability

Category: Security Compliance and Governance for AI Solutions
QuestionText: A company wants to track all invocations of foundation models across their organization for cost allocation and compliance. Which AWS service provides this visibility?
Explanation: Amazon Bedrock model invocation logging (via CloudWatch Logs and S3) captures detailed information about each model invocation including the model ID, input/output tokens, latency, and the IAM principal that made the call. Combined with AWS Cost Explorer tags, this provides comprehensive visibility into who is using which models, how much, and at what cost.
Option1: Enable Amazon Bedrock model invocation logging to CloudWatch Logs and S3 combined with cost allocation tags
Option2: Use Amazon GuardDuty for model usage tracking
Option3: Use AWS Config for invocation monitoring

Category: Security Compliance and Governance for AI Solutions
QuestionText: Which of the following is a security risk specific to generative AI applications?
Explanation: Prompt injection is a security risk unique to generative AI where malicious input manipulates the model into ignoring its instructions, revealing system prompts, or generating harmful content. Traditional applications don't have this attack vector. Mitigation includes input validation, Bedrock Guardrails, output filtering, and limiting the model's access to sensitive systems.
Option1: Prompt injection attacks that manipulate the model into unintended behavior
Option2: SQL injection attacks on databases
Option3: DDoS attacks on web servers

Category: Security Compliance and Governance for AI Solutions
QuestionText: A company wants to ensure their generative AI application cannot discuss competitors' products or make claims about pricing. Which Amazon Bedrock feature should they configure?
Explanation: Bedrock Guardrails denied topics feature allows you to define specific topics the model should not discuss. You provide a description of each denied topic (e.g., 'competitor products', 'pricing commitments'), and Guardrails blocks responses that touch on these topics. This prevents the AI from making unauthorized claims or discussing off-limits subjects.
Option1: Configure denied topics in Amazon Bedrock Guardrails
Option2: Fine-tune the model to forget about competitors
Option3: Use IAM policies to block competitor-related queries

QuestionText: A company wants to translate text between languages in their application. Which AWS service should they use?
Explanation: Amazon Translate is a neural machine translation service that delivers fast, high-quality, affordable, and customizable language translation. It supports 75+ languages and can translate text in real-time or batch mode. Custom Terminology allows you to define how specific terms (brand names, product names) should be translated, ensuring consistency.
Option1: Amazon Translate
Option2: Amazon Comprehend
Option3: Amazon Lex

QuestionText: What is the purpose of a confusion matrix in machine learning?
Explanation: A confusion matrix is a table that visualizes the performance of a classification model by showing the counts of true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives. It provides a complete picture of where the model is making correct predictions and where it is making errors, enabling calculation of precision, recall, F1 score, and accuracy.
Option1: A table showing true positives true negatives false positives and false negatives to evaluate classification model performance
Option2: A matrix used to confuse the AI model during training
Option3: A data structure for storing training data

QuestionText: What is 'model distillation' in the context of foundation models?
Explanation: Model distillation trains a smaller 'student' model to mimic the behavior of a larger 'teacher' model. The student learns from the teacher's outputs rather than the original training data. The resulting smaller model is faster and cheaper to run while retaining much of the larger model's capability. Amazon Bedrock supports model distillation for creating efficient custom models.
Option1: Training a smaller model to replicate the behavior of a larger model for faster and cheaper inference
Option2: Removing water from the model's training data
Option3: Compressing model files for storage

QuestionText: Which of the following BEST describes the difference between a foundation model and a traditional ML model?
Explanation: Foundation models are trained on broad, diverse data and can perform many tasks through prompting or fine-tuning (general-purpose). Traditional ML models are trained on specific datasets for specific tasks (purpose-built). A foundation model can summarize text, translate, classify, and generate code—a traditional model typically does one thing. This versatility is what makes foundation models foundational.
Option1: Foundation models are general-purpose trained on broad data; traditional ML models are purpose-built for specific tasks
Option2: Foundation models are always smaller than traditional ML models
Option3: Traditional ML models are more versatile than foundation models

QuestionText: A company wants to use Amazon Bedrock to process customer emails and automatically draft responses. Which of the following should they include in their system prompt? (Select TWO)
Explanation: The company's tone and communication guidelines ensure responses match the brand voice (professional, friendly, formal). Instructions to never make commitments or promises the company can't keep prevent the AI from overstepping its authority (like promising refunds without authorization). Both are essential system prompt components for customer-facing AI applications.
Option1: The company's tone and communication style guidelines
Option2: Instructions to never make unauthorized commitments or promises
Option3: The model's internal architecture details

QuestionText: What is the difference between Amazon Bedrock and Amazon SageMaker JumpStart for accessing foundation models?
Explanation: Bedrock is a fully managed serverless service for building generative AI applications—no infrastructure management. SageMaker JumpStart provides foundation models that you deploy to SageMaker endpoints, giving you more control over the infrastructure (instance types, scaling, networking) but requiring more operational management. Bedrock is simpler; JumpStart offers more customization.
Option1: Bedrock is serverless with no infrastructure management; SageMaker JumpStart deploys models to managed endpoints with more infrastructure control
Option2: They are identical services with different names
Option3: SageMaker JumpStart only supports Amazon models

QuestionText: A company discovers that their AI hiring tool is rejecting a disproportionate number of candidates from a specific demographic group. What should they do?
Explanation: All of these actions are necessary. Immediately pausing the tool prevents further harm. Investigating the root cause (biased training data, proxy variables, algorithmic bias) identifies what went wrong. Implementing bias mitigation (retraining with balanced data, removing biased features, adding fairness constraints) fixes the issue. Ongoing monitoring prevents recurrence. This is a comprehensive responsible AI response.
Option1: Immediately pause the tool and investigate the root cause of the bias
Option2: Retrain the model with balanced representative data and bias mitigation techniques
Option3: Implement ongoing monitoring for demographic disparities

QuestionText: What is the 'right to explanation' in the context of AI and regulations like GDPR?
Explanation: Under GDPR and similar regulations, individuals have the right to receive meaningful information about the logic involved in automated decisions that significantly affect them. This means organizations using AI for decisions like loan approvals, hiring, or insurance pricing must be able to explain how the AI reached its decision in terms the individual can understand.
Option1: The right of individuals to receive an explanation of how automated AI decisions that affect them were made
Option2: The right of AI companies to keep their algorithms secret
Option3: The right to use AI without any restrictions

Category: Security Compliance and Governance for AI Solutions
QuestionText: A company wants to implement a generative AI application that processes healthcare data. Which compliance consideration is MOST important?
Explanation: HIPAA compliance is critical for any application processing Protected Health Information (PHI). Amazon Bedrock is HIPAA-eligible, meaning it can be used in architectures that process PHI when proper safeguards are in place (BAA with AWS, encryption, access controls, audit logging). The company must ensure their entire architecture—not just the AI component—meets HIPAA requirements.
Option1: Ensuring HIPAA compliance for processing Protected Health Information (PHI)
Option2: Ensuring the application has a modern user interface
Option3: Using the newest available foundation model

Category: Security Compliance and Governance for AI Solutions
QuestionText: Which of the following are ways to control costs when using Amazon Bedrock? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Provisioned Throughput provides committed capacity at discounted rates for predictable workloads. Setting up AWS Budgets with alerts notifies you when Bedrock spending approaches or exceeds defined thresholds, preventing unexpected costs. Both are important cost governance mechanisms for enterprise AI deployments.
Option1: Use Provisioned Throughput for predictable workloads at discounted rates
Option2: Set up AWS Budgets with alerts for Bedrock spending thresholds
Option3: Use the most expensive model for all tasks

QuestionText: Which of the following is TRUE about Amazon SageMaker?
Explanation: All of these are true. SageMaker provides built-in algorithms (XGBoost, Linear Learner, etc.) so you don't need to write ML code from scratch. It supports custom training with your own algorithms in Docker containers. SageMaker provides managed Jupyter notebooks for development and experimentation. It is a comprehensive ML platform covering the entire lifecycle.
Option1: It provides built-in algorithms for common ML tasks
Option2: It supports custom training with your own algorithms
Option3: It provides managed Jupyter notebooks for development

QuestionText: What is 'continued pre-training' and how does it differ from fine-tuning?
Explanation: Continued pre-training extends the model's general knowledge by training on a large domain-specific corpus (e.g., medical literature, legal documents) using the same unsupervised objective as the original pre-training. Fine-tuning adapts the model to specific tasks using labeled examples (input-output pairs). Continued pre-training teaches the model new knowledge; fine-tuning teaches it new behaviors.
Option1: Continued pre-training extends domain knowledge with large unlabeled corpora; fine-tuning adapts behavior with labeled task-specific examples
Option2: They are the same process with different names
Option3: Continued pre-training is cheaper than fine-tuning

QuestionText: A company wants to build a generative AI application that generates SQL queries from natural language questions about their database. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: A foundation model can translate natural language to SQL (text-to-SQL) when provided with the database schema in the prompt. The prompt includes the table definitions, column descriptions, and relationships, along with the natural language question. The model generates the corresponding SQL query. Few-shot examples of question-SQL pairs improve accuracy for complex queries.
Option1: Provide the database schema in the prompt and use a foundation model to generate SQL from natural language questions
Option2: Train a custom model from scratch for SQL generation
Option3: Use Amazon Athena's built-in natural language interface

QuestionText: Which of the following statements about Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases is TRUE?
Explanation: Bedrock Knowledge Bases manages the entire RAG pipeline automatically. It ingests documents from S3, chunks them, generates embeddings using a selected embedding model, and stores them in a vector database (OpenSearch Serverless, Aurora, Pinecone). At query time, it retrieves relevant chunks and provides them as context to the foundation model. All of this is fully managed.
Option1: It automatically handles document ingestion chunking embedding generation vector storage and retrieval for RAG
Option2: It requires you to manage the vector database infrastructure
Option3: It only works with Amazon Titan models

QuestionText: Which of the following is an example of a potential harm from generative AI that organizations should mitigate?
